phase1.explanation:
🎉 Congratulations! You've completed Unit 3 — Exception Handling & File I/O! Let's review everything and look at the big picture.

--- Unit 3 Recap ---

1. Exceptions — handling unexpected events
   • Checked (compile-time) vs Unchecked (runtime)
   • try-catch-finally structure
   • throw (create) vs throws (declare)

2. Custom Exceptions — domain-specific errors
   • Extend Exception (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked)
   • Meaningful error messages

3. try-with-resources — automatic resource cleanup
   • AutoCloseable interface
   • No more finally blocks for closing

4. Built-in Exceptions — know your enemies
   • NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
   • ClassCastException, NumberFormatException
   • ArithmeticException, IllegalArgumentException
   • IOException, FileNotFoundException
   • InterruptedException

5. Byte Streams — raw binary data
   • FileInputStream, FileOutputStream
   • Reading/writing bytes
   • Best for binary files (images, audio, etc.)

6. Character Streams — text data
   • FileReader, FileWriter
   • Reading/writing characters
   • Best for text files (handles encoding)

7. Buffered Streams — performance boost
   • BufferedReader, BufferedWriter
   • readLine(), newLine()
   • Wrapping pattern

8. PrintWriter — formatted output
   • println(), printf()
   • Autoflush option
   • Formatted tables

9. File Class — file operations
   • Exists, create, delete, rename
   • List directory contents
   • File metadata

10. Serialization — save/restore objects
    • Serializable interface
    • transient fields (not serialized)
    • ObjectInputStream/ObjectOutputStream

11. NIO — modern file handling
    • Path interface
    • Files utility class
    • readAllBytes(), readAllLines()

12. CSV — data exchange format
    • Reading/writing CSV files
    • Splitting and parsing fields
    • Handling edge cases

--- Defensive Programming Mindset ---

Always assume the worst:
• Files might not exist
• Input might be invalid
• Network might fail
• Memory might be low

Best practices:
1. Validate all inputs
2. Handle exceptions gracefully
3. Provide meaningful error messages
4. Use try-with-resources
5. Log errors (not just print)
6. Fail fast with clear messages
7. Don't ignore exceptions (empty catch blocks)

--- What's Next? Unit 4 — Collections & Threads ---

You'll learn:
• ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet
• Generics
• Sorting and searching
• Multithreading basics
• Concurrency

You're building real, robust applications now! 💪
phase1.openingDialogue: Unit 3 complete! You've made your programs robust and reliable! 🛡️

phase3.prompt:
Which practice makes a program MORE robust?

A) Using empty catch blocks to ignore all exceptions
B) Checking for null before accessing objects
C) Reading one byte at a time from a large file
D) Not closing files after use
E) Using System.out.println() for all errors

Select the BEST practice.
dialogueHints: Think about defensive programming — what prevents errors?
What's the most common mistake that causes NullPointerException?
Which practice actively prevents problems from occurring?
phase3.openingDialogue: Final check! What makes code robust? 🤔
